evals.prompt: Test my local Vite app: start the dev server, open the homepage, wait for it to settle, click the login button, and tell me about any console errors or broken interactions you see.
evals.expected_output: The response uses the local-webapp testing workflow rather than ad hoc browser automation. It starts the server safely, performs rendered-state reconnaissance after the page settles, drives the requested interaction, and reports browser findings.
expectations: It uses the `with_server.py` helper or equivalent managed server lifecycle rather than leaving a stray dev server running.
It waits for the page to settle, such as `networkidle`, before choosing selectors and interacting.
It performs rendered-state inspection instead of guessing selectors from source alone.
It captures and reports relevant console errors or browser-visible failures.
It runs Chromium headless and closes the browser cleanly.

evals.prompt: I have a static HTML file with a form in it. Use Playwright to load it, fill the fields, submit it, and verify the success banner. Treat it like a static file, not a whole web app stack.
evals.expected_output: The response handles the page as a static HTML target rather than inventing unnecessary app-server steps. It loads the file, inspects the rendered DOM, drives the form, and verifies the success state.
expectations: It treats the target as a static HTML case rather than assuming a full dev-server workflow.
It uses Playwright to inspect the rendered DOM before automating the form interaction.
It verifies the success state in the rendered UI rather than assuming submission worked.
It keeps the browser run headless and self-contained.

evals.prompt: There is already a server running on localhost:3000. Inspect the rendered page first, figure out the right selectors, submit the search form, and tell me what changed in the DOM and browser logs.
evals.expected_output: The response uses the existing running server rather than starting a duplicate one. It performs reconnaissance first, then automates the interaction and reports both DOM changes and browser-side findings.
expectations: It does not start a duplicate server when a running server is already supplied.
It performs rendered-state reconnaissance before choosing selectors.
It automates the requested interaction and reports the observed DOM change.
It reports relevant browser logs or console errors from the run.

evals.prompt: Start my local app, reproduce the disappearing toast after login, record a video of the repro, and tell me where the video file was saved along with any console errors.
evals.expected_output: The response treats the task as a timing-sensitive bug repro, enables video recording before page creation, preserves browser logs, and returns a concrete saved video artifact path.
expectations: It recognizes that a transient toast bug is a good reason to record a video.
It configures video on `browser.new_context(...)` with `record_video_dir` before creating the page.
It keeps the browser headless instead of switching to a headed fallback by default.
It calls `context.close()` so the video artifact is fully written.
For a human-facing recording, it adds pointer tracking with an orange dot and click feedback with a ripple effect.
It reports the final video path explicitly, ideally via `video.save_as(...)` or an equivalent saved-path workflow.
It still reports console errors or browser-side findings instead of treating the recording as sufficient by itself.

evals.prompt: Use the running app on localhost:3000, click through the UI to the profile settings screen, record a walkthrough video, and tell me where the video file was saved.
evals.expected_output: The response records a user-facing walkthrough from the existing running app, reaches the destination through the UI rather than guessing a deep link, and reports a saved video artifact path.
expectations: It does not start a second server when the app is already running.
It treats this as a UI walkthrough case where recording a video is appropriate.
It records video via Playwright context settings such as `record_video_dir` and `record_video_size`.
It adds pointer tracking with an orange dot and click feedback with a ripple effect so the walkthrough is readable.
It navigates through the UI instead of assuming a direct settings URL.
It uses `context.close()` and reports the saved video path explicitly to the user.
